skill.name: azure-resource-visualizer
skill.description: Analyze Azure resource groups and create Mermaid architecture diagrams that show resources, layers, dependencies, and data flows.
skill.summary: Create readable Azure architecture diagrams from resource group inventory.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious code, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or weak cryptographic implementation. Most hits are markdown code fences, Mermaid diagram syntax, KQL examples, and private sample IP ranges. The remaining risk is legitimate but elevated: the skill instructs agents to query Azure resources with MCP tools or az, which can expose sensitive cloud metadata.

medium_findings.title: Read-only Azure CLI and MCP command use
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to query Azure resources with MCP tools or az commands. This is aligned with the skill purpose, but it can run external tooling and return sensitive cloud inventory data.
medium_findings.recommendation: Publish with a warning that users should approve Azure CLI execution, keep commands read-only, and avoid interpolating untrusted names into shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly instruct use of az or MCP-generated Azure commands. The commands are for legitimate inventory work, so this is not malicious but remains an external command risk.

medium_findings.title: Collection of sensitive Azure resource metadata
medium_findings.description: The workflow asks the agent to capture resource names, network settings, identity information, dependencies, and configuration details. This metadata can be sensitive when copied into generated documentation.
medium_findings.recommendation: Warn users to review diagrams and markdown output before sharing, especially when resource names, network ranges, identities, or dependency details are included.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill intentionally performs broad resource inventory and relationship mapping. This is expected behavior, but the collected metadata can disclose infrastructure details.

low_findings.title: Markdown and Mermaid backticks misclassified as Ruby execution
low_findings.description: Several static external command hits occur inside markdown code fences, Mermaid examples, KQL snippets, or inline formatting. These are documentation examples, not Ruby backtick execution paths.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is required for markdown fences. Keep reviewing actual CLI examples separately from formatting syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding content is markdown documentation and Mermaid or KQL syntax. There is no executable Ruby file or runtime path in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Private sample IP ranges misclassified as network endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP findings are RFC1918 example address ranges shown inside a sample Azure virtual network diagram. They are not external endpoints or exfiltration destinations.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is required. Continue using non-routable sample ranges for documentation examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are 10.0.0.0/16 and related private subnets in a Mermaid example. They cannot identify a public command-and-control or exfiltration endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are documentation false positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography hits appear on prose, Mermaid labels, headings, placeholders, or KQL syntax. No cryptographic function or weak algorithm implementation was found.
low_findings.recommendation: No cryptography remediation is needed. The scanner should treat Mermaid, KQL, and markdown prose differently from executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage in the cited files. The flagged terms are part of documentation and diagram syntax.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance wording describes intended Azure inventory
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hits refer to listing Azure resource groups, discovering resources, and validating diagram output. This is the core purpose of the skill, not local host reconnaissance.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep the marketplace warning focused on cloud metadata exposure rather than treating the documentation as host reconnaissance malware.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill repeatedly frames discovery as Azure resource inventory for architecture documentation. No evidence found of local system probing, persistence, or stealth behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure command generation and execution path
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows use of Azure CLI and MCP command generation for resource discovery. This is necessary for the feature but should be constrained to read-only operations.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Require user consent before running generated commands and reject write operations such as create, update, delete, assign, or deploy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command path is explicit and legitimate. Risk remains because generated or parameterized CLI commands can access sensitive Azure inventory.

content.value_statement: Azure resource groups can be hard to understand when services, networks, identities, and dependencies are spread across many views. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create clear Mermaid architecture diagrams from Azure inventory.
actual_capabilities: Lists or validates Azure resource groups before analysis.
Queries resource details through Azure MCP tools or Azure CLI.
Maps relationships across compute, network, data, identity, and monitoring resources.
Generates Mermaid diagrams with subgraphs, labeled edges, and configuration details.
Creates markdown architecture documentation with inventory tables and notes.
limitations: Requires Azure access through MCP tools or an authenticated Azure CLI session.
Does not create, modify, delete, or deploy Azure resources.
Does not perform security scanning or performance diagnostics.
Large resource groups may need multiple diagrams for readability.

use_cases.title: Document a resource group
use_cases.description: Create a readable architecture document for an existing Azure resource group.

use_cases.title: Review dependencies before migration
use_cases.description: Map service relationships before moving, refactoring, or consolidating Azure workloads.

use_cases.title: Explain infrastructure to stakeholders
use_cases.description: Produce a diagram that shows major services, data flows, and network boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Diagram one resource group
prompt_templates.prompt: Visualize the Azure resource group named <resource-group>. Create a Mermaid architecture diagram and a short markdown summary.

prompt_templates.title: Choose from available groups
prompt_templates.prompt: List my available Azure resource groups, ask me which one to analyze, then create an architecture diagram for my selection.

prompt_templates.title: Focus on network and identity
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze <resource-group> and create a diagram focused on virtual networks, subnets, private endpoints, managed identities, and Key Vault access.

prompt_templates.title: Create layered architecture documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a complete architecture document for <resource-group>. Include inventory, Mermaid diagrams by layer, relationship details, and sharing notes.

output_examples.input: Visualize the production web app resource group.
output: A markdown architecture document with the resource group name, region, and resource count.
A Mermaid diagram grouped into network, compute, data, security, and monitoring layers.
A short explanation of key dependencies and data flows.

output_examples.input: Show network relationships for an Azure workload.
output: A focused diagram showing virtual networks, subnets, network security groups, apps, and databases.
Labeled connections that explain subnet placement, rules, and private access paths.

best_practices: Use read-only Azure permissions when generating diagrams.
Review generated markdown before sharing it outside your team.
Split very large environments into diagrams by layer or workload.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to deploy or change Azure resources.
Do not publish diagrams that reveal sensitive names, IP ranges, or identities.
Do not assume resource relationships when Azure data is incomplete.

faq.question: Does this skill modify Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. The instructions define read-only analysis and explicitly exclude creating, modifying, or deleting Azure resources.

faq.question: What access does it need?
faq.answer: It needs access to Azure MCP tools or an authenticated Azure CLI session with permission to list and read resources.

faq.question: What format does it generate?
faq.answer: It generates markdown documentation with Mermaid diagrams, inventory tables, relationship details, and notes.

faq.question: Can it analyze multiple subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes, it includes Azure Resource Graph guidance for cross-subscription inventory when permissions allow it.

faq.question: Is it a security scanner?
faq.answer: No. It may note architecture observations, but it is not designed for vulnerability scanning or compliance review.

faq.question: What should users check before sharing output?
faq.answer: Users should check for sensitive resource names, private network ranges, identity details, and dependency information.
